Event details

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Saturday, March 14, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

From SEAtoday - All Events

Event details

item.title: Saturday Flow + Yin Yoga
item.description:
Saturday, March 14, 2026 at 8 AM

This class will leave you feeling physically and energetically balanced! Practice begins with 30 minutes of dynamic movement. Emphasis on synchronizing breath with poses to create an intense internal heat and purifying sweat that detoxifies the body. The last 30 minutes of practice, poses are held for several minutes focusing on finding increased mobility in the joints – spine, hips and shoulders. Each pose will aim to access the deep layers of fascia to release tension holding areas on the body. \*Drop-in for $25

From SEAtoday - All Events

Event details

item.title: The Lives We Transform Annual Auction
item.description:
Saturday, March 14, 2026 at 8 AM

Location: Embassy Suites, 20610 44th Ave W, Lynnwood, WA

Save the Date! More details available on the Clothes For Kids website! The Lives We Transform Auction is Clothes For Kids’ signature annual fundraising event, bringing together community members, donors, and supporters for an evening of generosity, celebration, and impact. Held on Saturday, March 14, 2026, at the Embassy Suites in Lynnwood, WA, the event features exciting silent and live auctions, a delicious dinner, and heartfelt stories highlighting the difference your support makes. All proceeds directly support our mission to provide free, high-quality school wardrobes to local students in need—helping them feel confident, prepared, and ready to thrive. Join us in transforming lives, one wardrobe at a time!

item.title: Highway 9 Bridge Planting Party
item.description:
Saturday, March 14, 2026 at 9 AM

Description Join us at this restoration site along South Skagit Highway near the Highway 9 bridge. From 9am-12pm we'll plant native trees and shrubs to restore riparian habitat at this riverside site. These plants will help restore the Skagit River and improve habitat for salmon and many other animals. Bring a club, group and all your friends- all ages welcome! Please dress for the weather, and wear shoes that can get muddy. We'll provide gloves, tools and hot drinks/snacks! Click the link to register today!

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Saturday, March 14, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

Event details

item.title: Introduction to Orchid Care and Repotting
item.description:
Saturday, March 14, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Volunteer Park Conservatory, 1400 East Galer Street, Seattle, WA

Introduction to Orchids and their care. The orchid family \(Orchidacea\) is one of the largest and most diverse plant families. It possibly represents 10% of the flowering plant species. There are between 20,000 and 35,000 species \(about 800 genera\) and can be found on every continent except Antarctica. There are 41 native terrestrial orchid species in Washington State. Orchids are characterized by having highly specialized flower adaptations usually for pollination by insects. This class will cover the physical characteristics of orchids, care and feeding of the plants. Each student is encourages to bring up to 3 of their plants for evaluation and possible repotting. The lecture and demonstration portion of the class will be one hour followed by the evaluation and repotting demonstrations for an hour.

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Saturday, March 14, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

From SEAtoday - All Events

Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Saturday, March 14, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Sunday, March 15, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

From SEAtoday - All Events

item.title: Sermon Series: 1 Peter
item.description:
Sunday, March 15, 2026 at 9:30 AM

This winter, from January to Easter, we are walking through 1 Peter at Woodmark Church. Some of you know the book well. For others, it may feel unfamiliar. But Peter names the tension every believer feels. We live in a world that often moves in a different direction. He calls us “exiles,” yet reminds us we are chosen, loved, held by a living hope. The heart of this series is simple. It is not just about studying a letter. It is about becoming the kind of people Peter envisions: Steady. Courageous. Formed by grace. Join us each Sunday, 9:30 am and 11:30 am in Kirkland.

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Sunday, March 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

Shop in-person at our Bellevue locations for expert tuning & repair, custom boot fitting, water sports rentals, and more: Sturtevant's – 13131 NE 20th St., Bellevue, WA 98005 \(425.869.7669\) Hours: Mon-Fri 10 am-7 pm, Sat-Sun 10 am- 6 pm Sturtevant's Ski Mart – 13219 NE 20th St., Bellevue, WA 98005 \(425.637.8958\) Hours: Mon-Fri 10 am-7 pm, Sat-Sun 10 am-6 pm Or head to Tacoma: Sturtevant's Ski Mart – 2220 So. 37th St., Tacoma, WA 98409 \(253.473.1134\) Hours: Mon-Fri 10 am-7 pm, Sat-Sun 10 am-6 pm. Also shop online at christysports.com or sturtevants.com. Upgrade your gear locally this spring—visit Sturtevant's Bellevue or Tacoma today. item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Sunday, March 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

From SEAtoday - All Events

Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Sunday, March 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

From SEAtoday - All Events

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Sunday, March 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

From SEAtoday - All Events

Event details

item.title: Kameelah Janan Rasheed: we leak, we exceed
item.description:
Sunday, March 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Kameelah Janan Rasheed \(b. 1985, East Palo Alto, CA; lives and works in Brooklyn, NY\) is a self-described “learner” who creates multi-media works that explore the poetics-pleasures-politics of Black knowledge production, information technologies, \[un\]learning, and belief formation. She often integrates found images and words alongside her own writing to create collaged wall installations and experimental video works. Across her work, Rasheed investigates the revision and life cycles of text, embracing expanded and embodied forms of writing and reading. we leak, we exceed will activate the unique volume and multiple vantage points of the Henry’s double-height gallery, drawing together threads from physics, Black critical thought, and information theory to create an immersive environment that interrogates the spatial and social implications of compression. A common process used in data storage, spatial organization, and information systems, compression abbreviates and collapses complex ideas into more simplified forms. Rasheed questions the way compression comes at the cost of nuance and creates unrecoverable losses. She draws parallels between the compression of information and the containment of people, both physically and through the structuring and defining of identities. Through a network of video, sound, and architectural mark-making, Rasheed proposes alternatively what she calls “an embrace of Black excess and expansion” as a liberatory practice.

From SEAtoday - All Events

Event details

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Sunday, March 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

item.description:
Sunday, March 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Sturtevant's Ski Mart, 13219 NE 20th St., Bellevue, WA

PNW skiers and snowboarders—spring is the smartest time to upgrade! Sturtevant's \(part of Christy Sports\) is hosting the annual Ski-Ya-Later Sale from March 6-29, offering up to 50% off current-season skis, snowboards, boots, bindings, apparel, and more—perfect as spring storms bring late-season conditions and families plan ahead during spring break. Shop in-person at our Bellevue locations for expert tuning & repair, custom boot fitting, water sports rentals, and more: Sturtevant's – 13131 NE 20th St., Bellevue, WA 98005 \(425.869.7669\) Hours: Mon-Fri 10 am-7 pm, Sat-Sun 10 am- 6 pm Sturtevant's Ski Mart – 13219 NE 20th St., Bellevue, WA 98005 \(425.637.8958\) Hours: Mon-Fri 10 am-7 pm, Sat-Sun 10 am-6 pm Or head to Tacoma: Sturtevant's Ski Mart – 2220 So. 37th St., Tacoma, WA 98409 \(253.473.1134\) Hours: Mon-Fri 10 am-7 pm, Sat-Sun 10 am-6 pm. Great for families: Stop by for kids' fit checks, season rental info, and early purchases before fall pricing. All discounted items are final sale; selection varies by store. Also shop online at christysports.com or sturtevants.com. Upgrade your gear locally this spring—visit Sturtevant's Bellevue or Tacoma today. Dates: March 6 – March 29, 2026 \(Progressive discounts: Up to 30% off March 6-12 with the best selection; up to 40% off March 13-19; up to 50% off March 20-29 for the deepest savings\)

Event details

item.title: Young Dragon: A Bruce Lee Story
item.description:
Sunday, March 15, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Seattle Children's Theatre, 201 Thomas Street, Seattle, WA

Before he was a legend, Bruce Lee was just a young man trying to figure it all out—right here in Seattle. This action-packed, soul-searching story follows the Young Dragon as he navigates a new country and finds his voice. Through movement, magic, and heart, we meet the human behind the icon—flawed, fierce, and full of possibility. Watch as Lee learns to clear his brain by mimicking the white noise of water and begins fusing martial arts with philosophy. From street fights in Hong Kong to teaching self-defense and chasing big dreams, Bruce’s journey reminds us that finding your way means first figuring out who you are. Come see how Seattle shaped him into a flexible, fluid, and flowing master—and how he reshaped the world.

From SEAtoday - All Events

Event details

item.title: Boren Banner Series: Camille Trautman
item.description:
Sunday, March 15, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Frye Art Museum, 704 Terry Ave, Seattle, WA

Every photograph is a reminder that the act of framing is never neutral. In Camille Trautman’s first solo museum exhibition in their hometown, the Seattle-born Duwamish artist uses photography and video to challenge colonial narratives and counter Indigenous erasure. The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

From SEAtoday - All Events

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Monday, March 16, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

From SEAtoday - All Events

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Monday, March 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

From SEAtoday - All Events

item.title: Susan Meiselas: Crossings
item.description:
Monday, March 16, 2026 at 12 PM

Location: Photographic Center Northwest (PCNW), 900 12th Ave., Seattle, WA

First presented at the Art Institute of Chicago in 1990, this exhibition by Susan Meiselas intertwines documentary photographs of Central America during the 1970s and 1980s \(from Nicaragua and El Salvador\) along with panoramic images from the US/Mexico border taken in 1989. Additional photographs of the US/Mexico border taken in 2018 are also featured in a site-specific gallery installation. The exhibition invites viewers to see immigration in the context of economic and political crises in the region. Today, the work feels newly urgent. US immigration enforcement has expanded through militarization and constant surveillance, with leaders using fear-driven language to divide communities and legitimize brutal forms of control. These photographs ask us to look carefully, to recognize familiar patterns, and to hold on to our shared humanity across the globe. People move in search of safety, possibility, and a better life for their families, which is a universal hope that crosses borders, generations, and time.

From SEAtoday - All Events

item.title: Inside the Clerkship
item.description:
Monday, March 16, 2026 at 4 PM

Location: Sullivan Hall, 110, Seattle, WA

Join us for an exclusive and candid panel discussion featuring distinguished judges and former and current law clerks who will demystify the clerkship application process. Designed for law students at all stages, this event offers firsthand insight into what judges look for in applicants, how to stand out in a competitive pool, and how to strategically prepare throughout law school. Panelists will share practical advice on building strong relationships with faculty, crafting compelling application materials, navigating interviews, and positioning yourself for both state and federal clerkships. Attendees will also gain valuable perspective on what life is like inside chambers and how a clerkship can shape long-term career trajectories. In addition, this program will preview the Federal Litigation course offered in Fall 2026. Attendees will learn how the course strengthens the research, writing, and advocacy skills that judges value most in clerkship candidates, and how it can position students for success in competitive judicial hiring. Whether you're just beginning to consider a clerkship or preparing to apply, this program will provide actionable guidance, insider tips, and an opportunity to ask questions directly to those involved in the hiring process—while offering a forward-looking glimpse at how the upcoming Federal Litigation course can support your goals. Panelists: Judge Roopali Desai, United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit Judge Kymberly Evanson, United States District Court for the Western District of Washington Judge Jamal Whitehead, United States District Court for the Western District of Washington Luther Reed-Caulkins, Current Judicial Law Clerk to the Honorable Kymberly Evanson Ruby Nagamine, Current Judicial Law Clerk to the Honorable Richard A. Jones, United States District Court for the Western District of Washington Dalvin Yarbrough, Former Judicial Law Clerk for The Honorable Reggie B. Walton, United States District Court for the District of Columbia, 2023-2025 Moderator: Cynthia Jones, Jones Legal Group and Adjunct Professor, Seattle University School of Law \(Federal Litigation\) Date: March 16th, 2026 Time: 4:00 PM to 5:30 PM Location: Sullivan Hall, Room 110

item.title: Food and Cooking Through Science
item.description:
Monday, March 16, 2026 at 5 PM

Location: Seattle Central College Continuing Education, 1701 Broadway, Seattle, WA

Gain a deeper insight into what’s going on when you cook and bake! Whether you are a home cook or a professional, you can become a much better cook by learning the science of food and cooking. You'll fall in love with cooking, as well as the beauty of the amazing world we live in. Focus on understanding the fascinating field of food science through a combination of fun and fascinating lectures and demos. Learn to make better choices about cooking methods and techniques, and how to produce better finished products more easily and consistently. Elevate your knowledge about nutrition, how food works, sustainability issues, and the chemistry of cooking and baking. Develop modern techniques using scientific principles. Learn many fundamental concepts and apply them to real-world cooking and baking.

item.title: Issaquah Drink N' Draw
item.description:
Monday, March 16, 2026 at 6 PM

Location: Formula Brewing, 1875 NW Poplar Way, Issaquah, WA

ISSAQUAH'S LOCAL ART MEETUP Calling all artists and art enthusiasts! Join us for an evening filled with creativity, camaraderie, and, of course, drinks at your local Issaquah Drink n' Draw! Location: Formula Brewing, 1875 NW Poplar Way, Issaquah, WA 98027 Indulge your artistic side in a relaxed and social setting. Whether you're a seasoned artist or just starting your creative journey, this event is the perfect opportunity to: Draw: Bring your sketchbooks, pencils, tablets, or any medium of your choice. As long as it can fit in a 2' x 2' space and can be cleaned up easily, we welcome art of all types! Unleash your imagination and create alongside like-minded individuals. Let your creativity flow! Don't know what to draw? Search the hashtag \#essdrawmonthly or find the latest pinned Prompt List on the ESS group page! ️ Chat: Engage in lively conversations with fellow artists. Share tips, techniques, and stories while getting inspired by each other's work. Connect with a diverse community passionate about art. Drink: Sip on your favorite beverages from Formula's selection of refreshments. Whether it's a cold beer, a glass of wine, or a non-alcoholic option, enjoy a drink while you immerse yourself in the creative process. ✏️ This event is open to artists of all skill levels and backgrounds. Feel free to join us solo or invite friends to share in this artistic experience. Admission is FREE. Seating is first-come, first-served. While there's usually enough table space, if we get too busy, you may have to spread out. Come and be a part of this vibrant artistic gathering! Let's sketch, chat, and create memorable art together. We can't wait to see your masterpieces! For more information, contact group organizers Blayne Fox and Kat Klockow. Note: Remember to drink responsibly and be mindful of others' space and creativity during the event.

From SEAtoday - All Events

Event details

item.title: Liveable Kirkland Meetup -- Project: Build Whistle Kits
item.description:
Monday, March 16, 2026 at 6 PM

Location: Chainline Brewing Company, 500 Urban Plz, Kirkland, WA

Liveable Kirkland Meetups Mon, Feb 16, Mar 2, Mar 16, 6-8pm Chainline Brewing at Kirkland Urban For this special series of Meetups, we'll kick off our usual socials with an ICE-breaker activity -- assembling whistle kits. These kits will contain a tiny whistle, a "Know Your Rights" card, and instructions on what to do and who to call if you see ICE Activity. After assembling whistle kits, we'll socialize as usual. We'll get to know one another and chat about local events and issues, probably including things like homes for everyone, sustainability, people-oriented communities, and ways to improve the quality of life in Kirkland for everyone. Drop in and bring friends, family, and neighbors! Okay to bring kids and food, too. Follow all of Liveable Kirkland's events: On our calendar: https://www.liveablekirkland.org/calendar On Meetup: https://www.meetup.com/liveablekirkland/ And connect with us on Discord, Facebook, and subscribe to our weekly newsletter here: https://www.liveablekirkland.org/join

Event details

item.title: Smarty Pints! Tree Care and Big Data Astronomy
item.description:
Monday, March 16, 2026 at 6:30 PM

Location: Burke-Gilman Brewing Company, 3626 NE 45th St, Suite 102, Seattle, WA

Spring is coming, and with it the occasional appearance of that weird big blue cloud! On March 16 we’re looking towards the sky, and beyond, with talks on maple trees and care \(timely!\) and the search for life beyond Earth \(never not timely!\). See you at 6:30 in the taproom! 🌳 Zach McCurry ISA Certified Arborist, Emerald Arbor Care Arborist Tells All: The Secret Life of a Broadleaf Maple The secret life of trees revealed, the newest scientific field, and how it affects you in Seattle. Learn how an arborist sees your trees, how to care for them, & what good tree work is all about. 👽 Prof. James Davenport Research Assistant Professor, UW Astronomy Director, DiRAC Institute Searching for ET with ‘big data’ astronomy New wide field sky surveys, backed with powerful computational systems, allow us to start new and exciting ways of asking the age old question: Are we alone? Kick back, look up, and ponder the trees and the stars \(but recline after Smarty Pints, we need space for you to sit!\)

item.title: EMBODIED ALTARS DANCE CLASS
item.description:
Monday, March 16, 2026 at 6:30 PM

Location: Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center, 5011 Bernie Whitebear Way, Seattle, WA

EMBODIED ALTARS is an all-levels dance class hosted by Daybreak Star Radio. Designed by Indigenous Chicana artist and El Sueño director, Alicia Mullikin. Dancers will utilize Indigenous principles within movement, meditation, and song as a way to practice gratitude, honor the land, and recognize their ancestors. The class is for those who are interested in reflection, intention setting, and a discovery of their own stories. No dance experience necessary. Class Time: Mondays, 6:30-8PM No class 9/15/25 No class 10/13/25 Venue: Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center 5011 Bernie Whitebear Way, Seattle, WA 98199 Sliding Scale: $10-$20\* per class. Sign Up Now!   This venue is ADA accessible w/ ADA bathrooms and a wheelchair lift to the second floor. Class will be held in the ballroom space, this area has 5 ADA parking spaces. There are an additional 25 parking spots in our lot, there is ample parking space along the road leading up to Daybreak star as well as the Discovery Park North Parking lot. What to bring: water, comfortable clothes to dance in, journal, your personal identity, a desire to explore new pathways and an open mind/heart. Hosted at Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center Produced by United Indians of All Tribes Foundation In Partnership with El Sueno Dance & Daybreak Star Radio “Indigenize the Airwaves” “Art. Culture. Reclamation.” \*Talk to us about BIPOC and Queer/LGBTQIA2S\+ Scholorships\* Please contact @el.sueno.dance on IG for private conversations about this topic.

Event details

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Tuesday, March 17, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: BE LOUD Breakfast
item.description:
Tuesday, March 17, 2026 at 8 AM

Description Join us for the BE LOUD Breakfast, an annual fundraising event for the King County Sexual Assault Resource Center \(KCSARC\). This year&'s event is especially meaningful as 2026 marks the 50th anniversary of KCSARC. For fifty years, KCSARC has empowered survivors and communities. This incredible milestone is a testament to you-our community. You have shown up, spoken out, and stood with survivors every step of the way. Today, we face new and urgent challenges. But our mission remains unwavering. Together, we will keep moving forward-LOUDER than ever before. Will you BE LOUD with us? Register now at donate.kcsarc.org/BELOUD26

item.title: Coworking Tuesdays
item.description:
Tuesday, March 17, 2026 at 9:30 AM

Location: Bothell Kenmore Chamber, 10031 Main Street, Suite A, Bothell, WA

Tuesday Cowork \+ Coffee/Tea \(Weekly\) Need a reason to get out of your inbox cave and into a productive, low-pressure space? Join us every Tuesday for a drop-in coworking morning—bring your laptop, knock out your priorities, and enjoy the energy of working alongside other local professionals. When: Tuesdays | 9:30–11:30 AM | No registration needed Format: Come for the full two hours or pop in when you can Who it’s for: Business owners, remote workers, creatives, students, nonprofit leaders—anyone who wants focused work time \+ light community What to expect: Quiet “get-it-done” work blocks, optional hellos \+ quick intros, and a friendly vibe \(no forced networking\) Location \(through February\): Bothell Visitor Center We’ll rotate locations monthly—same time, same Tuesday rhythm, fresh spaces. Bring: Your laptop \+ whatever you need to work. We have the coffee, tea, and water! Questions? Email sandra@bothellkenmorechamber.org

item.title: March Madness Watch Party at Five Iron Golf - Seattle Capitol Hill
item.description:
Tuesday, March 17, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Five Iron Golf - Seattle Capitol Hill, 1525 11th Ave Suite 100, Seattle, WA

Five Iron Golf is your College Basketball HQ this March! Gather your crew and book 1–2 simulator bays for up to 12 people \(6 people per bay\) for a bracket-busting watch party experience. Enjoy all-day TV coverage of college basketball alongside cutting-edge golf and multisport simulators, a locally-inspired menu, and custom beverage packages. Book your watch party for March 19 or 20 and you'll automatically be entered to win a 20-person happy hour with drinks and appetizers — a $1,000 value! Group packages can include reserved event space, team-specific décor, a dedicated staff member, full-screen and/or picture-in-picture viewing, a customizable menu, beverage packages, free club rentals, multisport simulators, and table games. Select the "Small Event" option at booking to ensure simulator bays are placed side-by-side. Availability of select features varies by location. Promotions: Happy Hour – "It's 5i o'Clock Somewhere" \(Monday–Friday, 3–6 PM\) Enjoy $5 bottomless boneless wings, $10 bottomless bone-in wings, $5 select beers/cans/cocktails, $5 dips \(guacamole, hot honey ricotta, hummus\), half-off pizzas, and $5 featured shots. Dine-in only at participating locations; simulator rental may be required. 5i After Dark – "Swing In, For Less"  \(Sunday–Thursday 9 PM to close | Friday–Saturday 10 PM to close\) Features $29/hr simulator rentals plus the same great food and drink specials as Happy Hour, with wings priced per dozen rather than bottomless. Dine-in only at participating locations; simulator rental may be required.

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Tuesday, March 17, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

From SEAtoday - All Events

Event details

item.title: Susan Meiselas: Crossings
item.description:
Tuesday, March 17, 2026 at 12 PM

Location: Photographic Center Northwest (PCNW), 900 12th Ave., Seattle, WA

First presented at the Art Institute of Chicago in 1990, this exhibition by Susan Meiselas intertwines documentary photographs of Central America during the 1970s and 1980s \(from Nicaragua and El Salvador\) along with panoramic images from the US/Mexico border taken in 1989. Additional photographs of the US/Mexico border taken in 2018 are also featured in a site-specific gallery installation. The exhibition invites viewers to see immigration in the context of economic and political crises in the region. Today, the work feels newly urgent. US immigration enforcement has expanded through militarization and constant surveillance, with leaders using fear-driven language to divide communities and legitimize brutal forms of control. These photographs ask us to look carefully, to recognize familiar patterns, and to hold on to our shared humanity across the globe. People move in search of safety, possibility, and a better life for their families, which is a universal hope that crosses borders, generations, and time.

From SEAtoday - All Events

Using 5″ x 7” photocopies of your own garden photos, illustrations, or journal pages, you will learn how to transfer your image onto wood panels, tiles, or stone using melted beeswax. You will bring your own photocopies; all additional equipment and materials will be provided. $31.50 BBGS Member/$45 Non-Member

item.title: City Council Regular Hybrid Meeting
item.description:
Tuesday, March 17, 2026 at 5 PM

January 29, 20206 - Notice is hereby given that the Mercer Island City Council will hold a public hearing at its Hybrid Meeting on Tuesd ay, March 3, 2026, at approximately 5pm, to receive comments on Ordina nce No. 26-02, renewing interim regulations in MICC 19.16.010 related to emergency shelters and housing, transitional housing, and permanent supportive housing in response to E2SHB 1220. The public hearing will b e held in person and using Zoom. The public will have the opportunity to c omment during the public hearing by either attending in person, calling in, or logging onto the meeting via Zoom. Written comments may be submitted to the City of Mercer Island by e-mail to council@mercerisland.gov until such time that the public hearing is adjourned. Detailed instruction s on how to comment live during the public hearing will be available onlin e on or before February 25, 2026, at: https://www.mercerisland.gov/citycouncil. Andrea Larson, CMC City Clerk Notice: City Council Calendar - January 29, 2026 City Council Agenda Distribution List - January 29, 2026 Website Public Notice - January 29, 2026 Mercer Island Reporter - January 28, 2026

item.title: Baking Fundamentals: Cookies, Cakes, Custards and More
item.description:
Tuesday, March 17, 2026 at 5 PM

Location: Seattle Central College Continuing Education, 1701 Broadway, Seattle, WA

Would you like to be able to make a variety of beautiful and delicious baked treats with confidence? Gain fundamental skills and knowledge for baking in this hands-on class. We will learn about ingredients, interactions, and techniques, using a variety of mixing methods, recipes, and best practices. And we will make a broad range of yummy foods, such as muffins, biscuits, sponges, chiffons, angel food cakes, quiche, pâte à choux, custards, crèmes, pastry doughs, pies, tarts, layer cakes, frostings, meringue, buttercreams, and specialty desserts. Enjoy making delectable baked goods, both sweet and savory. We will also discuss alternative ingredients for people with restrictive diets, food sensitivities, and food allergies. Take advantage of the large, well-equipped kitchen at Seattle Central's Culinary Academy. Learn some of the most useful tips from an experienced chef, and develop practices for working in a clean, safe, and efficient way.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Wednesday, March 18, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Wednesday, March 18, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

item.title: Sturtevant's Ski-Ya-Later Sale: Up to 50% Off Ski and SnowboardGear
item.description:
Wednesday, March 18, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Sturtevant's Ski Mart, 13219 NE 20th St., Bellevue, WA

PNW skiers and snowboarders—spring is the smartest time to upgrade! Sturtevant's \(part of Christy Sports\) is hosting the annual Ski-Ya-Later Sale from March 6-29, offering up to 50% off current-season skis, snowboards, boots, bindings, apparel, and more—perfect as spring storms bring late-season conditions and families plan ahead during spring break. Shop in-person at our Bellevue locations for expert tuning & repair, custom boot fitting, water sports rentals, and more: Sturtevant's – 13131 NE 20th St., Bellevue, WA 98005 \(425.869.7669\) Hours: Mon-Fri 10 am-7 pm, Sat-Sun 10 am- 6 pm Sturtevant's Ski Mart – 13219 NE 20th St., Bellevue, WA 98005 \(425.637.8958\) Hours: Mon-Fri 10 am-7 pm, Sat-Sun 10 am-6 pm Or head to Tacoma: Sturtevant's Ski Mart – 2220 So. 37th St., Tacoma, WA 98409 \(253.473.1134\) Hours: Mon-Fri 10 am-7 pm, Sat-Sun 10 am-6 pm. Great for families: Stop by for kids' fit checks, season rental info, and early purchases before fall pricing. All discounted items are final sale; selection varies by store. Also shop online at christysports.com or sturtevants.com. Upgrade your gear locally this spring—visit Sturtevant's Bellevue or Tacoma today. Dates: March 6 – March 29, 2026 \(Progressive discounts: Up to 30% off March 6-12 with the best selection; up to 40% off March 13-19; up to 50% off March 20-29 for the deepest savings\)

item.title: March Madness Watch Party at Five Iron Golf - Seattle Capitol Hill
item.description:
Wednesday, March 18, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Five Iron Golf - Seattle Capitol Hill, 1525 11th Ave Suite 100, Seattle, WA

Five Iron Golf is your College Basketball HQ this March! Gather your crew and book 1–2 simulator bays for up to 12 people \(6 people per bay\) for a bracket-busting watch party experience. item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Wednesday, March 18, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

From SEAtoday - All Events

Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Wednesday, March 18, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

From SEAtoday - All Events

Event details

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Wednesday, March 18, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

From SEAtoday - All Events

Event details

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Wednesday, March 18, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

item.title: Trail Explorers - Kelly's Brook Trail
item.description:
Wednesday, March 18, 2026 at 10:30 AM

Location: Farmer's Market: 245 Freshwater Road. Upper parking lot, 4749 Ballard Ave NW, Seattle, WA

Join the Trail Explorers on Wednesday, March 18, at 10:30 a.m. They will be exploring Kelly's Brook Trail, and the meeting location is the Farmer’s Market: 245 Freshwater Road. Upper parking lot. This trail is 4 km and will take approximately 60-75 minutes to complete. It is rated a 3 on the difficulty scale \(on a 1-5 scale, with 1 being the easiest and 5 being the hardest. For more information please visit: https://www.stjohns.ca/en/recreation-culture/trail-explorers.aspx This initiative provides a wonderful opportunity to explore the beautiful trails throughout the city, promoting health, wellness, and community connection. Please contact healthyprograms@stjohns.ca for further information.

item.title: Working Together to Protect and Transfer Assets - VIRTUAL WEBINAR
item.description:
Wednesday, March 18, 2026 at 11 AM

Description ️ Working Together to Protect and Transfer Assets Presented by: Peggy Haslach & Patrice Kreider-Hughes Location: GSBA Office Date & Time: March 18 11am - 12:30pm Join GSBA for an informative session designed to help LGBTQ\+ individuals, couples, and families protect what matters most. Working Together to Protect and Transfer Assets brings together a financial planner and an estate planning attorney to show how collaboration ensures your wishes are honored, your assets are protected, and your loved ones are cared for. What You'll Learn How to build a trusted planning team that understands LGBTQ\+ financial and legal needs Strategies to protect assets during your lifetime with coordinated insurance, investments, and legal planning Essential documents: durable powers of attorney, healthcare directives, wills, and trusts Aligning beneficiary designations with legal structures for seamless asset transfer Best practices across diverse family structures and varying state laws Featured Speakers Peggy Haslach, CFP, CLU Financial Planner working with women professionals and small business owners Peggy built her practice serving women professionals \(including veterinarians, doctors, and attorneys\) and small business owners. After seeing first-hand how life events impact women's financial security, she pivoted careers at 53-focused on collaborative, educational planning for women and LGBTQ\+ clients. Her clients are forward-thinking women who've put the pieces together-business, life, goals, beliefs, and savings-but want a cohesive plan they fully understand. Outside of work, Peggy competes nationally in dog agility and shares that passion with her wife, Desiree, a national and world champion handler and trainer. They live in Sumner, Washington with their three Border Collies-Vista, Cru, and Peak. Patrice M. Kreider-Hughes, Esq. Founder & Principal, Kreider Hughes Law, PLLC Patrice is an estate planning and small-business attorney with 15\+ years of experience. She earned her B.A. in psychology from the University of Virginia and her J.D. from Seattle University School of Law. Before founding Kreider Hughes Law in 2017, she served as in-house counsel for Conover Insurance, handling mergers and acquisitions, contracts, and employment matters. Featured on the Seattle Connected podcast and recognized by Expertise.com in 2023 and 2024, Patrice lives in Seattle with her wife, their daughter Simone, and pets Luther and Emma. Session Outline Why Collaboration Matters Financial planners and estate attorneys working in tandem to build resilient, values-aligned plans. Building Your Planning Team Roles, responsibilities, and coordination between advisors who understand LGBTQ\+ needs. Protecting Assets During Your Lifetime Insurance \(life, disability, long-term care\), investment strategy \(diversification, ESG\), and ownership/titling alignment. Legal Documents That Safeguard Your Wishes Durable POA, healthcare POA & advance directive, wills & trusts, and syncing beneficiary designations. Coordinating Asset Transfers Real estate titling, retirement & insurance beneficiaries, business succession, and philanthropic giving. Navigating Legal Landscapes & Family Structures Marriage recognition, parental rights, cross-border/visa considerations, and state-by-state variability. Q&A \(15 min\) - Bring your questions on real estate, trusts, chosen family, and children's inheritance planning. Who Should Attend LGBTQ\+ individuals, couples, and families seeking long-term financial security Small business owners planning for succession and legacy Anyone looking to align legal and financial strategies

From SEAtoday - All Events

Event details

item.title: Boren Banner Series: Camille Trautman
item.description:
Wednesday, March 18, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Frye Art Museum, 704 Terry Ave, Seattle, WA

Every photograph is a reminder that the act of framing is never neutral. In Camille Trautman’s first solo museum exhibition in their hometown, the Seattle-born Duwamish artist uses photography and video to challenge colonial narratives and counter Indigenous erasure. The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

From SEAtoday - All Events

item.title: Susan Meiselas: Crossings
item.description:
Wednesday, March 18, 2026 at 12 PM

Location: Photographic Center Northwest (PCNW), 900 12th Ave., Seattle, WA

First presented at the Art Institute of Chicago in 1990, this exhibition by Susan Meiselas intertwines documentary photographs of Central America during the 1970s and 1980s \(from Nicaragua and El Salvador\) along with panoramic images from the US/Mexico border taken in 1989. Additional photographs of the US/Mexico border taken in 2018 are also featured in a site-specific gallery installation. The exhibition invites viewers to see immigration in the context of economic and political crises in the region. Today, the work feels newly urgent. US immigration enforcement has expanded through militarization and constant surveillance, with leaders using fear-driven language to divide communities and legitimize brutal forms of control. These photographs ask us to look carefully, to recognize familiar patterns, and to hold on to our shared humanity across the globe. People move in search of safety, possibility, and a better life for their families, which is a universal hope that crosses borders, generations, and time.

item.title: UW Psychology Distinguished Alumni Lecture, Feat. Dr. Cynthia Berg
item.description:
Wednesday, March 18, 2026 at 3:30 PM

Location: UW Hans Rosling Center for Population Health, 3980 15th Ave NE, Seattle, WA

University of Washington Department of Psychology - Distinguished Alumni Lecture, Feat. Dr. Cynthia Berg A Self- and Social-Regulation Perspective on Type 1 Diabetes Management Across the Life Span Type 1 diabetes is a difficult illness to manage and requires daily self-regulation \(e.g., planning to meet diabetes goals, self-efficacy, and managing emotions\) as well as social regulation \(disclosing to others to obtain needed assistance\). Although aspects of parent involvement in diabetes management decline across adolescence and into young adulthood, our longitudinal studies show that having family members and romantic partners who are knowledgeable about one’s diabetes behaviors and are available to help when needed is associated with better diabetes outcomes. Social regulation can also facilitate more effective use of technologies such as continuous glucose monitors by sharing GM data with others. These studies have served as the foundation for randomized clinical trials that hold promise for optimizing self- and social-regulation on a daily basis for individuals across the life span.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Thursday, March 19, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: Audubon Bird Walk
item.description:
Thursday, March 19, 2026 at 9 AM

Location: Bellevue Botanical Garden, 12001 Main St, Bellevue, WA

Learn more about the birds at the Bellevue Botanical Garden. Join Eastside Audubon for a monthly walk that explores the birds and their habitat in the Garden. This one-mile walk will be on trails with various surfaces and may include gravel, mulch, or pavement. Please dress for the weather and wear comfortable walking shoes. Parking in the lot is free. From the parking lot enter the garden through the breezeway and turn left. Meet your guide in the covered Gathering Place. This walk is for any level of birder and no experience is necessary. Bring binoculars if you have them. There will be some available to loan for the walk. Each walk is $5 per person. Registration is required.

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Thursday, March 19, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.
